brief:
The fixed income market is sending a clear message this week, and it is not a comfortable one for anyone with duration on their books. The front end has decisively broken through the repo rate floor — the 91D at 9.85% and 364D at 10.07% are pricing in rate cuts that Bangladesh Bank has not yet signalled — while the long end remains stubbornly elevated. But the real story lies in the belly, where the curve has fractured in ways that demand immediate attention from every ALCO desk in the country.

Three distortions define this market. First, the 2Y at 11.95% sits a staggering 113 basis points above the 5Y at 10.82% — the most violent belly inversion we have seen in this cycle. This is not normal repricing; it reflects a market where banks are dumping 2Y paper or where a specific issuance has overwhelmed demand at that tenor. Either way, the 2Y is broken and should be avoided until the dislocation resolves. Second, the 15Y at 12.68% trades 103 basis points above the 20Y at 11.65%, creating an ultra-long kink that no rational expectations model can explain. Third, even the bill segment shows strain — the 182D at 10.20% trades through the 364D at 10.07%, a 13bp inversion that suggests front-end supply pressure. When a curve exhibits three simultaneous inversions across different segments, the signal is unambiguous: the market is under structural stress, and smooth interpolation-based risk models are giving you the wrong answers.

The fundamental backdrop presents a paradox that your lending and investment committees must confront. Deposit growth at 12% far exceeds credit growth at 7.1%, flooding the system with ৳3.35 lakh crore in excess liquidity — the highest in years. But this liquidity is not deployable. Over 70% sits in government securities because NPLs at 30.5% have made banks deeply risk-averse to fresh lending. Meanwhile, the government borrowed ৳62,000 crore net in the first eight months of FY26 — nearly five times the year-ago level — and the fiscal year-end borrowing surge has not even begun. With revenue collection at just 44.9% of target and ADP implementation at a historic low of 11.5%, expect an avalanche of government paper in Q4 that will pressure the long end further.

The positioning recommendation is straightforward but requires discipline. Concentrate new investments in the 91D–364D segment where bid-covers remain healthy (1.75x–1.92x), devolvement risk is nil, and you earn positive carry over call money at 9.80%. If you must extend duration, the 5Y at 10.82% is the sweet spot — it cleared cleanly at auction, offers 82 basis points over repo, and sits below the broken 2Y. Avoid the 2Y entirely until the inversion normalises. The 10Y at 11.15% is tempting optically, but the 10% devolvement and 1.4x bid-cover at the last auction tell you that demand is not there yet. On FX reserves at $35.1 billion with BB now a net USD buyer, the external buffer is the strongest it has been in two years — one genuine bright spot. The April inflation print is your key catalyst: any break below 9% reopens the easing conversation and could draw real money into the 10Y+ segment. Until then, stay short, stay liquid, and let the curve come to you.
